questions.stem: Accounting standards are written policy documents covering which four aspects of a transaction or event?

options.text: Planning, recording, budgeting and forecasting
options.explanation: Budgets and forecasts sit outside the financial statements standards govern.

options.text: Recognition, measurement, presentation and disclosure
options.explanation: The canonical four: when to record, at what amount, where to show it, and what to say about it.

options.text: Journalising, posting, balancing and closing
options.explanation: Those are bookkeeping steps, not the policy dimensions standards regulate.

options.text: Auditing, taxation, valuation and reporting
options.explanation: Standards govern financial reporting policy, not audit or tax procedure.

questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 1 §1 — meaning of accounting standards

questions.stem: The PRIMARY objective of accounting standards is to:

options.text: Minimise the tax liability of the reporting entity
options.explanation: Tax follows its own law; standards serve financial-statement users, not tax planning.

options.text: Replace the need for statutory audit
options.explanation: Audit and standards are complements — the auditor reports on compliance with the standards.

options.text: Standardise diverse accounting policies so that financial statements become comparable and transparent
options.explanation: Comparability across entities and periods, on a credible common basis, is the stated purpose.

options.text: Maximise the reported profit of compliant companies
options.explanation: Standards constrain reporting choices; they are indifferent to whether profit rises or falls.

questions.stem: The Accounting Standards Board was constituted:

options.text: By the ICAI, in 1977
options.explanation: The ICAI set up the ASB in 1977 to formulate accounting standards.

options.text: By SEBI, in 1992
options.explanation: 1992 is SEBI's own statute year — SEBI sits on the ASB but did not create it.

options.text: By the RBI, in 1977
options.explanation: The year is right but the parent is the ICAI, not the RBI.

options.text: By the Central Government, in 2013
options.explanation: 2013 is the Companies Act year; the ASB predates it by decades and belongs to the ICAI.

questions.stem: In the standard-setting procedure, the EXPOSURE DRAFT stage exists to:

options.text: Invite comments from the public before the standard is finalised
options.explanation: The exposure draft opens the proposal to public comment; the ASB finalises in light of the responses.

options.text: Let the tax department align the standard with the Income-tax Act
options.explanation: Tax alignment is not a stage of the ASB's process.

options.text: Notify the standard as law for companies
options.explanation: Notification is the Central Government's act under s.133, after the ICAI has issued the standard.

options.text: Test the standard on a sample of companies for one year
options.explanation: There is no pilot-testing stage in the procedure.

questions.stem: For COMPANIES, accounting standards acquire legal force when:

options.text: SEBI incorporates them into the listing agreement
options.explanation: Listing obligations ride on top of, and do not create, the s.133 standards.

options.text: The ASB approves the final draft
options.explanation: The ASB only formulates — it neither issues nor notifies.

options.text: The ICAI Council issues them
options.explanation: Council issuance binds ICAI members and non-corporate audits; companies need the s.133 notification.

options.text: The Central Government prescribes them under s.133, on ICAI's recommendation in consultation with the NFRA
options.explanation: s.133 is the notification route — currently exercised through the Companies (Accounting Standards) Rules, 2021.

questions.stem: A partnership firm's financial statements are governed by:

options.text: Ind AS, once the firm's turnover crosses ₹250 crore
options.explanation: The Ind AS roadmap and its thresholds apply to companies — a firm has no net-worth trigger.

options.text: No accounting standards at all
options.explanation: ICAI ASs reach non-corporates through the members who audit them.

options.text: The Companies (Accounting Standards) Rules 2021 directly
options.explanation: The 2021 Rules are notified under the Companies Act and bind companies, not firms.

options.text: The Accounting Standards issued by the ICAI, applied through its members who audit or report on them
options.explanation: Non-corporate entities follow ICAI-issued ASs; the s.133 notification route is for companies.

questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 1 §4 — status of accounting standards

questions.topic: Ch 1 · Intro to AS · Withdrawn AS
questions.stem: AS 6, Depreciation Accounting, was withdrawn because:

options.text: It was replaced by AS 26 on Intangible Assets
options.explanation: AS 26 retired AS 8 (R&D), not AS 6.

options.text: Depreciation ceased to be a mandatory charge
options.explanation: Depreciation remains mandatory — only its home standard changed.

options.text: Its content was absorbed into the revised AS 10, Property, Plant and Equipment
options.explanation: The AS 10 revision (from Fixed Assets to PPE) took depreciation with it, retiring AS 6.

options.text: It conflicted with the Income-tax Act
options.explanation: Tax depreciation always differed; that never withdrew the standard.

questions.topic: Ch 1 · Intro to AS · Withdrawn AS
questions.stem: AS 8, Accounting for Research and Development, stands withdrawn consequent to the issuance of:

options.text: AS 29, Provisions and Contingent Liabilities
options.explanation: AS 29 has nothing to do with research and development.

options.text: AS 26, Intangible Assets
options.explanation: AS 26 absorbed R&D accounting, retiring AS 8.

options.text: AS 10, Property, Plant and Equipment
options.explanation: Revised AS 10 retired AS 6 (depreciation), not AS 8.

options.text: AS 22, Accounting for Taxes on Income
options.explanation: AS 22 concerns deferred tax and never touched R&D.

questions.stem: Of the 29 Accounting Standards issued by the ICAI, the number effectively in force is:

options.text: 25, after four withdrawals
options.explanation: Only two withdrawals (AS 6 and AS 8) reduce the issued 29.

options.text: 27, after the withdrawal of AS 6 and AS 8
options.explanation: 29 issued − AS 6 (into revised AS 10) − AS 8 (into AS 26) = 27 standing.

options.text: 29, since withdrawal only suspends a standard
options.explanation: Withdrawal removes the standard from force — the effective count is 27.

options.text: 32, counting AS 30 to AS 32
options.explanation: The financial-instruments trio was never part of the notified rulebook and stands withdrawn by the ICAI.

questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 1 §3 — how many accounting standards

questions.stem: The accounting standards currently notified for AS-compliant companies are contained in:

options.text: The ICAI's Compendium of Guidance Notes
options.explanation: Guidance notes assist practice; they are not the statutory notification.

options.text: The Companies (Accounting Standards) Rules, 2006, as originally notified
options.explanation: The 2006 Rules stand superseded by the 2021 Rules.

options.text: The Companies (Indian Accounting Standards) Rules, 2015
options.explanation: The 2015 Rules notify IND AS, the converged rulebook — not the ASs this paper applies.

options.text: The Companies (Accounting Standards) Rules, 2021
options.explanation: Notified 23 June 2021, superseding the 2006 Rules (as amended in 2016).

questions.stem: Which of the following is a recognised LIMITATION of accounting standards?

options.text: They make financial statements comparable across entities
options.explanation: Comparability is a BENEFIT, not a limitation.

options.text: They give users a credible common basis of preparation
options.explanation: A common credible basis is precisely the advantage.

options.text: They reduce the scope for creative accounting
options.explanation: Constraining creative accounting is a benefit.

options.text: Where a standard permits alternative treatments, the choice between them restores some of the very divergence standards exist to remove
options.explanation: Permitted alternatives are the classic limitation, alongside rigidity and subordination to statute.

questions.stem: Where an accounting standard conflicts with a provision of an applicable Act:

options.text: The company may choose whichever treatment it prefers
options.explanation: There is no election — the statutory provision governs.

options.text: The standard prevails, being the more specialised rule
options.explanation: Specialisation does not trump legislation; the statute wins.

options.text: The Act prevails and the standard yields to that extent
options.explanation: Standards cannot override the statute — a recognised limitation of standard-setting.

options.text: The NFRA arbitrates case by case
options.explanation: No arbitration mechanism exists; the hierarchy is settled — statute first.

questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 1 §1 — standards cannot override statute

questions.topic: Ch 1 · Intro to AS · IFRS family
questions.stem: The body of literature loosely called 'IFRS' comprises:

options.text: IFRS and Ind AS taken together
options.explanation: Ind AS is India's CONVERGED rulebook, not a component of IFRS itself.

options.text: IFRS issued by the IASB, IAS issued by the IASC, and the IFRIC and SIC interpretations
options.explanation: The four-part family: current standards, inherited standards, and both generations of interpretations.

options.text: Only the standards issued after 2001 by the IASB
options.explanation: The inherited IAS and the interpretations are part of the package.

options.text: The IASB standards plus each country's national GAAP
options.explanation: National GAAPs sit outside IFRS — convergence bridges them, it does not merge them.

questions.stem: The International Accounting Standards (IAS) were issued by:

options.text: The IFRS Interpretations Committee
options.explanation: IFRIC writes interpretations, not standards.

options.text: The Financial Accounting Standards Board (FASB)
options.explanation: FASB writes US GAAP — a different lineage altogether.

options.text: The International Accounting Standards Committee (IASC), the IASB's predecessor
options.explanation: IAS predate 2001; the IASB adopted them and issues new standards as IFRS.

options.text: The International Accounting Standards Board (IASB)
options.explanation: The IASB issues IFRS; it inherited (not issued) the IAS.

questions.topic: Ch 1 · Intro to AS · Adoption vs convergence
questions.stem: India's approach to IFRS compliance is best described as:

options.text: Dual filing — every company reports under both IFRS and AS
options.explanation: No dual-filing regime exists; each company follows its one applicable rulebook.

options.text: Adoption — IFRS as issued by the IASB apply word for word
options.explanation: Verbatim application is adoption, which India did not choose.

options.text: Rejection — India retains only its own ASs
options.explanation: Ind AS are mandatory for large companies; India did not reject the IFRS framework.

options.text: Convergence — national standards (Ind AS) aligned with IFRS but with deliberate departures
options.explanation: India wrote IFRS-converged Ind AS with carve-outs/ins rather than adopting IFRS verbatim.

questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 1 §8, §10 — becoming IFRS compliant; convergence in India

questions.stem: In the Ind AS context, a CARVE-OUT is:

options.text: The withdrawal of an old AS on notification of Ind AS
options.explanation: AS withdrawals are a separate housekeeping matter, not carve-outs.

options.text: A departure that removes or changes an IFRS requirement considered unsuitable for Indian conditions
options.explanation: Carve-outs subtract or alter; carve-ins add options or guidance IFRS lacks.

options.text: An exemption from Ind AS for SME-exchange companies
options.explanation: The SME-exchange exemption is a roadmap rule, not a carve-out from IFRS text.

options.text: An addition to Ind AS of guidance that IFRS does not contain
options.explanation: That describes a CARVE-IN.

readLink.label: Read: Ind AS — convergence and roadmap →

questions.topic: Ch 1 · Intro to AS · Ind AS numbering
questions.stem: Ind AS numbering deliberately:

options.text: Uses years of issue as numbers
options.explanation: No rulebook numbers standards by year.

options.text: Continues the AS series from AS 30 onwards
options.explanation: Ind AS numbering follows the international family, not the old AS sequence.

options.text: Mirrors the corresponding IFRS/IAS number (e.g. Ind AS 109 ↔ IFRS 9, Ind AS 2 ↔ IAS 2)
options.explanation: The correspondence lets a reader line up the Indian and international texts instantly.

options.text: Is random, assigned in order of notification
options.explanation: The numbers are anything but random — they track IFRS/IAS.

readLink.label: Read: Ind AS — convergence and roadmap →

questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 1 §11, §13 — Ind AS and their numbering

questions.topic: Ch 1 · Intro to AS · Ind AS notification
questions.stem: Ind AS are notified under:

options.text: The ICAI's own resolution, with no government notification
options.explanation: For companies, legal force always comes from the s.133 notification.

options.text: The Companies (Indian Accounting Standards) Rules 2015, made under s.133
options.explanation: The 2015 Rules are the Ind AS notification, sharing the same s.133 anchor as the AS Rules.

options.text: The Companies (Accounting Standards) Rules 2021
options.explanation: The 2021 Rules notify the ASs — the other rulebook.

options.text: A SEBI circular binding listed companies
options.explanation: SEBI enforces but does not notify accounting standards.

readLink.label: Read: Ind AS — convergence and roadmap →

questions.stem: Under the Ind AS roadmap, VOLUNTARY adoption was available for financial years beginning on or after:

options.text: 1 April 2017
options.explanation: 1 April 2017 is Phase II.

options.text: 1 April 2018
options.explanation: 2018 belongs to the NBFC roadmap.

options.text: 1 April 2015, with comparatives
options.explanation: The voluntary window opened a year before Phase I's mandatory date.

options.text: 1 April 2016
options.explanation: 1 April 2016 is PHASE I's mandatory date, not the voluntary window.

readLink.label: Read: Ind AS — convergence and roadmap →

questions.stem: Phase I of the mandatory Ind AS roadmap (from 1 April 2016) covered:

options.text: All listed companies regardless of net worth
options.explanation: Remaining listed companies (below ₹500 crore) came in at PHASE II.

options.text: Listed (or listing-in-process) and unlisted companies with net worth of ₹500 crore or more, plus their holding, subsidiary, JV and associate companies
options.explanation: ₹500 crore is the Phase I line for both listed and unlisted companies, and group members ride along.

options.text: Unlisted companies with net worth of ₹250 crore or more
options.explanation: The ₹250-crore band belongs to Phase II (2017).

options.text: All NBFCs and banks
options.explanation: NBFCs, banks and insurers follow separate, later roadmaps.

readLink.label: Read: Ind AS — convergence and roadmap →

questions.stem: Companies listed ONLY on an SME exchange are, under the roadmap:

options.text: Not required to apply Ind AS — they continue with ASs unless they choose otherwise
options.explanation: The SME-exchange carve-out keeps them on the AS rulebook, with a voluntary door open.

options.text: Covered by Phase II like every other listed company
options.explanation: Phase II expressly excludes companies listed on SME exchanges.

options.text: Barred from ever adopting Ind AS
options.explanation: They MAY opt in voluntarily; they are just not compelled.

options.text: Required to apply Ind AS only in consolidated statements
options.explanation: No such split exists — they are simply outside the mandatory roadmap.

readLink.label: Read: Ind AS — convergence and roadmap →

questions.stem: Once a company begins applying Ind AS (voluntarily or mandatorily), it:

options.text: Must apply Ind AS for all subsequent financial statements — there is no going back
options.explanation: Ind AS adoption is a one-way door under the roadmap.

options.text: May revert if its net worth later falls below the threshold
options.explanation: Falling below the threshold does not release a company that has crossed over.

options.text: Must seek NFRA approval annually to continue
options.explanation: No annual approval mechanism exists — continuation is automatic and mandatory.

options.text: May revert to AS after five years
options.explanation: No sunset or revert window exists.

readLink.label: Read: Ind AS — convergence and roadmap →

questions.topic: Ch 1 · Intro to AS · NBFC roadmap
questions.stem: Under the separate NBFC roadmap, Ind AS first became mandatory (for NBFCs with net worth of ₹500 crore or more) from:

options.text: They remain outside Ind AS entirely
options.explanation: Large NBFCs have been on Ind AS since 2018-19.

options.text: 1 April 2015, voluntarily only
options.explanation: The 2015 voluntary window was for companies OTHER than banks, NBFCs and insurers.

options.text: 1 April 2016, with the corporate Phase I
options.explanation: NBFCs were expressly excluded from the corporate phases.

options.text: 1 April 2018, with comparatives
options.explanation: NBFC Phase I ran two years behind the corporate roadmap; listed and other specified NBFCs followed from 1 April 2019.

readLink.label: Read: Ind AS — convergence and roadmap →

questions.topic: Ch 1 · Intro to AS · Group drag-in
questions.stem: When a parent company falls under the Ind AS roadmap, its subsidiaries, associates and joint ventures:

options.text: Also apply Ind AS from the same date, even if individually below every threshold
options.explanation: Each phase expressly sweeps in the holding, subsidiary, associate and JV companies of covered entities.

options.text: Apply Ind AS only for the parent's consolidation workings
options.explanation: The obligation attaches to their own statutory financial statements, not merely consolidation packs.

options.text: May choose either framework independently
options.explanation: No election exists once the group trigger fires.

options.text: Continue on AS until they individually cross a threshold
options.explanation: The group drag-in overrides individual thresholds.

readLink.label: Read: Ind AS — convergence and roadmap →

questions.topic: Ch 1 · Intro to AS · This paper
questions.stem: The financial statements prepared throughout CA Intermediate Advanced Accounting comply with:

options.text: US GAAP
options.explanation: US GAAP has no place in the Indian statutory framework.

options.text: The Accounting Standards of the Companies (AS) Rules 2021 (and ICAI ASs for non-corporates)
options.explanation: This paper is an AS paper; the full Ind AS treatment belongs to the Final course.

options.text: Ind AS as notified in the 2015 Rules
options.explanation: Ind AS appear in this chapter only as orientation.

options.text: IFRS as issued by the IASB
options.explanation: No Indian statutory filing is made under raw IFRS.

readLink.label: Read: Which rulebook this paper uses →

questions.topic: Ch 1 · Intro to AS · Revised AS
questions.stem: Several ASs were revised in the 2021 Rules to align them, as far as possible, with:

options.text: The corresponding Ind AS
options.explanation: AS 2, 4, 10, 13, 14, 21 and 29 were revised to track their Ind AS counterparts where feasible.

options.text: US GAAP
options.explanation: US GAAP was never the alignment target.

options.text: The Income-tax computation provisions
options.explanation: Tax law (including ICDS) runs on its own track, separate from AS revisions.

options.text: The erstwhile AS 30-32 series
options.explanation: The withdrawn financial-instruments trio was not a template for revision.

questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 1 §3 — revisions in the 2021 Rules

questions.topic: Ch 1 · Intro to AS · Case: the birth of a standard
questions.case: Imagine the ICAI decides that a new area of accounting — say, cloud-computing arrangements — needs a standard for AS-compliant entities. Trace the journey: a body must formulate the draft, the public must get its say, someone must issue the final text, and for companies someone else must give it the force of law.

questions.stem: The body that identifies the area and prepares the draft standard is:

options.text: SEBI's accounting standards cell
options.explanation: SEBI participates in the ASB but does not run the drafting.

options.text: The NFRA
options.explanation: The NFRA is consulted at the notification stage; it does not draft.

options.text: The Ministry of Corporate Affairs
options.explanation: The MCA notifies for companies; drafting is the ASB's work.

options.text: The Accounting Standards Board of the ICAI
options.explanation: The ASB formulates: it picks the area, sets up study groups and prepares the draft.

questions.stem: Public participation in the standard's development happens through:

options.text: Parliamentary debate on the draft
options.explanation: Parliament legislates the Act, not individual accounting standards.

options.text: It does not — standards are drafted confidentially
options.explanation: The process is deliberately consultative; the exposure draft exists precisely for the public.

options.text: A referendum of ICAI members
options.explanation: There is no membership vote — comments on the exposure draft are the channel.

options.text: An exposure draft issued for comments
options.explanation: The exposure draft is the formal public-comment stage before finalisation.

questions.stem: The final standard is ISSUED (for the profession) by:

options.text: The Central Government
options.explanation: The Government NOTIFIES for companies; the professional issuance is the Council's.

options.text: The ASB directly
options.explanation: The ASB formulates and recommends — issuance is the Council's act.

options.text: The Council of the ICAI, after considering the ASB's final draft
options.explanation: The ASB submits to the Council, which modifies in consultation with the ASB and issues the standard.

options.text: The Comptroller and Auditor General
options.explanation: The CAG sits in the consultative circle but issues nothing.

questions.stem: For the standard to bind COMPANIES, the final step is:

options.text: Publication in the ICAI journal
options.explanation: Publication informs members; it creates no statutory obligation for companies.

options.text: Ratification by SEBI
options.explanation: SEBI enforces disclosure rules for listed entities but does not ratify accounting standards.

options.text: Notification by the Central Government under s.133, on ICAI's recommendation in consultation with the NFRA
options.explanation: s.133 turns the professional standard into law for companies — the route the AS Rules 2021 took.

options.text: Adoption by each company's board of directors
options.explanation: Boards comply with notified standards; they cannot choose to un-adopt them.

questions.topic: Ch 1 · Intro to AS · Case: Godavari group — which rulebook?
questions.case: Consider four companies at 1 April 2017, none of them banks, NBFCs or insurers: (1) Godavari Agro Ltd — unlisted, net worth ₹320 crore; (2) its wholly-owned subsidiary Godavari Seeds Ltd — unlisted, net worth ₹40 crore; (3) Tungabhadra Tools Ltd — listed only on an SME exchange, net worth ₹85 crore, no group ties to any Ind AS company; (4) Malaprabha Mills Ltd — unlisted, net worth ₹180 crore, no group ties, which voluntarily adopted Ind AS in FY 2015-16.

questions.stem: Godavari Agro Ltd (unlisted, net worth ₹320 crore) must:

options.text: Apply Ind AS from 1 April 2017 under Phase II (₹250 crore ≤ net worth < ₹500 crore)
options.explanation: Phase II's unlisted band is ₹250-500 crore, and ₹320 crore sits inside it.

options.text: Apply Ind AS from 1 April 2016 under Phase I
options.explanation: Phase I needs ₹500 crore; ₹320 crore waits for Phase II.

options.text: Choose either framework freely
options.explanation: Once a phase catches a company, Ind AS is mandatory, not optional.

options.text: Stay on AS — unlisted companies are outside the roadmap
options.explanation: Unlisted companies enter via the net-worth bands; ₹320 crore is caught.

questions.stem: Godavari Seeds Ltd (subsidiary, net worth only ₹40 crore) must:

options.text: Apply Ind AS only in the consolidation working papers
options.explanation: The obligation covers its own statutory financial statements.

options.text: Apply Ind AS along with its parent, despite being below every threshold
options.explanation: The roadmap sweeps in holding, subsidiary, associate and JV companies of covered entities.

options.text: Stay on AS until its own net worth crosses ₹250 crore
options.explanation: The group drag-in overrides individual size.

options.text: Seek an MCA exemption as a small subsidiary
options.explanation: No small-subsidiary exemption exists in the roadmap.

questions.stem: Tungabhadra Tools Ltd (listed only on an SME exchange) is:

options.text: In Phase I because it is listed
options.explanation: Phase I needed a ₹500-crore net worth; and SME-exchange companies are excluded from the sweeps anyway.

options.text: Outside the mandatory roadmap — it continues with AS unless it opts into Ind AS
options.explanation: SME-exchange listing is expressly excluded from Phase II's all-listed sweep.

options.text: In Phase II as a listed company
options.explanation: Phase II covers listed companies OTHER than those on SME exchanges.

options.text: Prohibited from voluntary adoption
options.explanation: The exemption is a shield, not a bar — voluntary adoption stays open.

questions.stem: Malaprabha Mills Ltd (net worth ₹180 crore, adopted Ind AS voluntarily in 2015-16) may now:

options.text: Never return to AS — voluntary adoption binds all subsequent financial statements
options.explanation: The no-looking-back rule applies equally to volunteers; size below the thresholds is irrelevant after crossing over.

options.text: Revert to AS since its net worth is below ₹250 crore
options.explanation: Falling below (or never reaching) a threshold does not unwind adoption.

options.text: Alternate frameworks year by year
options.explanation: Framework-hopping is precisely what the one-way rule forbids.

options.text: Revert with NFRA permission
options.explanation: No permission route back to AS exists.

questions.topic: Ch 1 · Intro to AS · Case: reading the international family
questions.case: A newly joined analyst is confused by the alphabet soup in a global group's accounting manual: it cites 'IAS 2', 'IFRS 9', an 'IFRIC' interpretation, and notes that the Indian subsidiary applies 'Ind AS 109 with a carve-out'. Help the analyst place each piece.

questions.stem: 'IAS 2' and 'IFRS 9' differ in that:

options.text: IAS are the Indian adaptations of IFRS
options.explanation: India's adaptations are the IND AS.

options.text: IFRS apply only in Europe while IAS apply worldwide
options.explanation: Geography does not divide them; issuing body and era do.

options.text: IAS were issued by the predecessor IASC and carried forward; IFRS are issued by the IASB itself
options.explanation: Same family, two generations — both remain in force within 'IFRS' broadly so called.

options.text: IAS are interpretations of IFRS
options.explanation: Interpretations are the IFRIC/SIC layer — IAS are full standards.

questions.stem: The 'IFRIC' document in the manual is:

options.text: An interpretation issued by the IFRS Interpretations Committee, part of the IFRS literature
options.explanation: IFRIC (and older SIC) interpretations round out the four-part IFRS family.

options.text: An audit guideline of the IAASB
options.explanation: Audit standards are a different body's output altogether.

options.text: A full standard superseding IFRS 9
options.explanation: Interpretations clarify standards; they do not supersede them.

options.text: A national carve-out document
options.explanation: Carve-outs live in national converged standards like Ind AS, not in IFRIC.

questions.stem: 'Ind AS 109 with a carve-out' tells the analyst that the Indian subsidiary applies:

options.text: A voluntary internal policy with no legal force
options.explanation: Ind AS are notified law for covered companies under the 2015 Rules.

options.text: India's converged counterpart of IFRS 9, departing from the IFRS text where Indian conditions demanded
options.explanation: Matching number (109 ↔ 9), converged content, with deliberate departures — the essence of convergence.

options.text: IFRS 9 exactly as issued by the IASB
options.explanation: Exact application would be adoption; the carve-out marks a departure.

options.text: The old AS series renumbered
options.explanation: Ind AS numbering tracks IFRS/IAS, not the AS series.

questions.stem: If the group instead ADDED an option in its Indian standard that IFRS does not offer, that addition is called:

options.text: An exposure draft
options.explanation: The exposure draft is a consultation stage, not a divergence label.

options.text: A carve-in
options.explanation: Additions are carve-ins; removals or changes to IFRS requirements are carve-outs.

options.text: A carve-out
options.explanation: Carve-outs subtract or modify — an ADDED option is a carve-in.

options.text: A SIC interpretation
options.explanation: SIC interpretations belong to the international family, not to national divergences.

questions.topic: Ch 1 · Intro to AS · Meaning & objectives
questions.stem: What are accounting standards? State their objectives and any two benefits. (4 marks)

skeleton.point: Written policy documents (expert body/government) covering recognition, measurement, presentation and disclosure of transactions and events
Objective: standardise diverse policies; comparable, transparent, credible financial statements
Benefits (any two): eliminates divergent practices; curbs creative accounting; inter-entity and inter-period comparability; reliable common basis for users

questions.stem: Discuss the limitations of accounting standards. (4 marks)

skeleton.point: Choice between permitted alternative treatments lets some divergence survive inside the standard
Rigidity: prescribed frames may fit unusual transactions poorly
Cannot override statute: where the Act prescribes a treatment, the Act prevails

questions.stem: Describe the procedure followed in formulating an accounting standard, and state how it becomes binding on companies. (5 marks)

skeleton.point: ASB (ICAI, 1977; broad composition) identifies the area and constitutes study groups for a preliminary draft
Draft circulated to Council members and specified bodies; exposure draft issued for public comment
ASB finalises considering comments; Council of ICAI modifies in consultation with ASB and issues the standard
For companies: Central Government notifies under s.133 on ICAI recommendation in consultation with NFRA (currently the AS Rules 2021)

questions.stem: How many accounting standards has the ICAI issued, and how many are effectively in force? Explain the difference. (4 marks)

skeleton.point: 29 accounting standards issued in all
AS 6 (Depreciation Accounting) withdrawn on revision of AS 10 into Property, Plant and Equipment
AS 8 (Accounting for R&D) withdrawn consequent to issuance of AS 26 (Intangible Assets)
Effectively 27 in force; several (AS 2, 4, 10, 13, 14, 21, 29) revised in the 2021 Rules towards their Ind AS counterparts

questions.stem: What does the term 'IFRS' comprise? Distinguish adoption from convergence. (5 marks)

skeleton.point: IFRS (broad sense) = IFRS issued by IASB + IAS issued by the predecessor IASC + IFRIC interpretations + SIC interpretations
Adoption: applying IFRS as issued by the IASB, verbatim, as the national requirement
Convergence: national standards aligned with IFRS but permitting departures for local law/conditions — India's route, via Ind AS with carve-outs/ins

questions.stem: Explain carve-outs and carve-ins in the context of Ind AS. Why do they exist? (4 marks)

skeleton.point: Carve-out: removal/modification of an IFRS requirement considered unsuitable for Indian conditions when framing the corresponding Ind AS
Carve-in: addition of an option or guidance in Ind AS that the IFRS text does not contain
They exist because India converged rather than adopted — departures accommodate local law, economic environment and practice

questions.stem: Summarise the Ind AS roadmap for companies other than banks, NBFCs and insurers. (6 marks)

skeleton.point: Voluntary: FY beginning 1 April 2015 or later, with comparatives; open to any such company and its group
Phase I (1 April 2016, mandatory): listed/in-process companies with net worth ≥ ₹500 crore; unlisted with net worth ≥ ₹500 crore; their holding/subsidiary/associate/JV companies
Phase II (1 April 2017, mandatory): all remaining listed companies except SME-exchange listings; unlisted with net worth ₹250-500 crore; plus group members
SME-exchange companies exempt unless they opt in; once Ind AS applies there is no reverting; uncovered companies stay on the notified ASs

questions.stem: Explain the status of accounting standards for companies and for non-corporate entities in India. (4 marks)

skeleton.point: Companies: standards notified by the Central Government under s.133 (AS Rules 2021 for AS-companies; Ind AS Rules 2015 for roadmap companies) are legally binding; s.129 requires compliance for a true and fair view
Non-corporates (firms, LLPs, trusts): ASs issued by the ICAI apply, enforced through members auditing/reporting on such entities
Auditors must report on compliance; deviations require disclosure and quantification
